name: Giotto
bio: Giotto di Bondone was an Italian painter and architect from Florence, working during the Gothic and Proto-Renaissance period. His contemporary, the banker and chronicler Giovanni Villani, called him 'the most sovereign master of painting in his time, who drew all his figures and their postures according to nature'. Giorgio Vasari described him as making a decisive break from the prevalent Byzantine style, initiating 'the great art of painting as we know it today' and introducing the technique of drawing accurately from life.
whyTheyMatter: Broke from the Byzantine style and founded the Western tradition of painting from nature, above all in his great fresco cycles.

name: Cimabue
bio: Giovanni Cimabue was an Italian painter and designer of mosaics from Florence. Although heavily influenced by Byzantine models, he is generally regarded as one of the first great Italian painters to break from the Italo-Byzantine style, giving his figures more lifelike proportions and using more sophisticated shading to suggest volume. According to Giorgio Vasari, Cimabue was the teacher of Giotto, the first great artist of the Italian Proto-Renaissance.
whyTheyMatter: One of the first great Italian painters to break from the Italo-Byzantine style, and by tradition the teacher of Giotto.

name: Duccio
bio: Duccio di Buoninsegna was an Italian painter active in Siena, Tuscany, in the late 13th and early 14th century, hired throughout his life to complete many important works in government and religious buildings around Italy. He is considered one of the greatest Italian painters of the Middle Ages, and is credited with creating the painting styles of the Trecento Gothic style and the Sienese school.
whyTheyMatter: Creator of the Sienese school and one of the greatest Italian painters of the Middle Ages.

name: Leonardo da Vinci
bio: Leonardo di ser Piero da Vinci was an Italian polymath of the High Renaissance, active as a painter, draughtsman, engineer, scientist, theorist, sculptor and architect. While his fame initially rested on his achievements as a painter, he has also become known for his notebooks, with drawings and notes on subjects from anatomy and astronomy to botany and cartography. He is widely regarded as a genius who epitomised the Renaissance humanist ideal, his collective works contributing to the development of European art to an extent rivalled only by his younger contemporary Michelangelo.
whyTheyMatter: The archetypal Renaissance genius, whose paintings and inquiries shaped European art like almost no other.

name: Michelangelo
bio: Michelangelo di Lodovico Buonarroti Simoni was an Italian sculptor, painter, architect and poet of the High Renaissance, born in the Republic of Florence and mostly active in Rome from his 30s onwards. His work was inspired by models from classical antiquity and had a lasting influence on Western art; he was lauded by contemporary biographers as the most accomplished artist of his era. His creative abilities and mastery across a range of artistic arenas define him as an archetypal Renaissance man, along with his rival and elder contemporary, Leonardo da Vinci.
whyTheyMatter: The most accomplished artist of his era, whose Sistine Chapel ceiling stands at the summit of the fresco tradition.

name: Raphael
bio: Raffaello Sanzio da Urbino, known in English as Raphael, was an Italian painter and architect of the High Renaissance. His work is admired for its clarity of form, ease of composition, and visual achievement of the Neoplatonic ideal of human grandeur. Together with Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo, he forms the traditional trinity of great masters of that period.
whyTheyMatter: One of the trinity of High Renaissance masters, whose Vatican fresco cycles defined classical grace and composition.

name: Titian
bio: Tiziano Vecellio, known in English as Titian, was an Italian Renaissance painter and the most important artist of Renaissance Venetian painting. One of the most versatile of Italian painters, he was equally adept with portraits, landscape backgrounds, and mythological and religious subjects. His painting methods, particularly in the application and use of colour, exerted a profound influence not only on painters of the late Italian Renaissance, but on future generations of Western artists.
whyTheyMatter: The supreme colourist of Venetian painting, whose handling of paint shaped generations of Western artists.

name: Correggio
bio: Antonio Allegri da Correggio was the foremost painter of the Parma school of the High Renaissance, responsible for some of the most vigorous and sensuous works of the sixteenth century. In his use of dynamic composition, illusionistic perspective and dramatic foreshortening, Correggio prefigured the Baroque art of the seventeenth century and the Rococo art of the eighteenth. He is considered a master of chiaroscuro.
whyTheyMatter: His illusionistic perspective and dramatic foreshortening in the domes of Parma prefigured Baroque ceiling painting.

name: Sandro Botticelli
bio: Alessandro di Mariano di Vanni Filipepi, better known as Sandro Botticelli, was an Italian painter of the Early Renaissance. His posthumous reputation suffered until the late 19th century, when he was rediscovered by the Pre-Raphaelites, who stimulated a reappraisal of his work. Since then, his paintings have been seen to represent the linear grace of late Italian Gothic and some Early Renaissance painting.
whyTheyMatter: Embodiment of Florentine linear grace, painter of The Birth of Venus and Primavera.

name: Jan van Eyck
bio: Jan van Eyck was an early Netherlandish painter active in Bruges, one of the early innovators of what became known as early Netherlandish painting and one of the supreme figures of the Early Northern Renaissance. Such was his legacy that he has been called 'the inventor of oil-painting' by Vasari, Ernst Gombrich and others, although this claim is now considered an oversimplification.
whyTheyMatter: Supreme figure of the Early Northern Renaissance, long credited as the inventor of oil painting.

name: Albrecht Dürer
bio: Albrecht Dürer was a German painter, printmaker and theorist of the German Renaissance. Born in Nuremberg, he established his reputation and influence across Europe in his twenties due to his high-quality woodcut prints. He was in contact with the major Italian artists of his time, including Raphael, Giovanni Bellini and Leonardo da Vinci, and from 1512 was patronized by Emperor Maximilian I.
whyTheyMatter: Carried Renaissance ideals north of the Alps and raised printmaking to a major art form.

name: Caravaggio
bio: Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio was an Italian painter active in Rome for a significant portion of his artistic life. He began his apprenticeship in Milan in 1584, and during the final four years of his life moved between Naples, Malta and Sicily. His paintings have been characterized by art critics as combining a realistic observation of the human state, both physical and emotional, with a dramatic use of lighting, which had a formative influence on Baroque painting.
whyTheyMatter: His dramatic lighting and unflinching realism had a formative influence on the whole of Baroque painting.

name: Annibale Carracci
bio: Annibale Carracci was an Italian painter and instructor, active in Bologna and later in Rome. Along with his brother Agostino and cousin Ludovico, he was one of the progenitors, if not founders, of a leading strand of the Baroque style, aspiring to a return to classical monumentality while adding a more vital dynamism. Painters working under Annibale at the gallery of the Palazzo Farnese would be highly influential in Roman painting for decades.
whyTheyMatter: His Farnese Gallery ceiling in Rome launched the grand tradition of Baroque fresco decoration.

name: Peter Paul Rubens
bio: Sir Peter Paul Rubens was a Flemish artist and diplomat, considered the most influential artist of the Flemish Baroque tradition. His highly charged compositions reference erudite aspects of classical and Christian history, and his immensely popular Baroque style emphasised movement, colour and sensuality, following the immediate, dramatic artistic style promoted in the Counter-Reformation. He produced altarpieces, portraits, landscapes and history paintings of mythological and allegorical subjects.
whyTheyMatter: The most influential master of Flemish Baroque, whose colour and movement echoed for centuries.

name: Diego Velázquez
bio: Diego Rodríguez de Silva y Velázquez was a Spanish Baroque painter, the leading artist in the court of King Philip IV of Spain and Portugal, and of the Spanish Golden Age. He is generally considered one of the greatest artists in the history of Western art.
whyTheyMatter: Court painter of the Spanish Golden Age, generally counted among the greatest artists in Western art.

name: Rembrandt
bio: Rembrandt Harmenszoon van Rijn was a Dutch Golden Age painter, printmaker and draughtsman. He is generally considered one of the greatest visual artists in the history of Western art. It is estimated that his surviving works amount to about three hundred paintings, three hundred etchings and several hundred drawings.
whyTheyMatter: The towering master of the Dutch Golden Age, generally considered one of the greatest visual artists in Western art.

name: Johannes Vermeer
bio: Johannes Vermeer was a Dutch painter who specialized in domestic interior scenes of middle-class life, and is considered one of the greatest painters of the Dutch Golden Age. During his lifetime he was a moderately successful provincial genre painter, recognized in Delft and The Hague; he produced relatively few paintings and primarily earned his living as an art dealer.
whyTheyMatter: Master of light and quiet domestic interiors, one of the greatest painters of the Dutch Golden Age.

name: Pietro da Cortona
bio: Pietro da Cortona was an Italian Baroque painter and architect. Along with his contemporaries and rivals Gian Lorenzo Bernini and Francesco Borromini, he was one of the key figures in the emergence of Roman Baroque architecture. He is best known for his frescoed ceilings, such as the vault of the salone of the Palazzo Barberini in Rome, and he carried out extensive painting and decorative schemes for the Medici family in Florence.
whyTheyMatter: His Palazzo Barberini vault defines High Baroque illusionistic ceiling painting.

name: Andrea Pozzo
bio: Andrea Pozzo was an Italian Jesuit brother, Baroque painter, architect, decorator, stage designer and art theoretician. He was best known for his grandiose frescoes using the technique of quadratura to create an illusion of three-dimensional space on flat surfaces. His masterpiece is the nave ceiling of the Church of Sant'Ignazio in Rome, and through his techniques he became one of the most noteworthy figures of the Baroque period.
whyTheyMatter: Supreme master of quadratura, the art of the illusionistic painted ceiling.

name: Giovanni Battista Tiepolo
bio: Giovanni Battista Tiepolo was an Italian painter and printmaker from the Republic of Venice who painted in the Rococo style, considered an important member of the 18th-century Venetian school. He was prolific, and worked not only in Italy, but also in Germany and Spain.
whyTheyMatter: The last great master of the grand illusionistic ceiling fresco, from Venice to Würzburg and Madrid.

name: Antoine Watteau
bio: Jean-Antoine Watteau was a French painter and draughtsman whose brief career spurred the revival of interest in colour and movement, as seen in the tradition of Correggio and Rubens. He revitalized the waning Baroque style, shifting it to the less severe, more naturalistic Rococo. Watteau is credited with inventing the genre of fêtes galantes, scenes of bucolic and idyllic charm suffused with a theatrical air.
whyTheyMatter: Invented the fête galante and turned the Baroque toward the Rococo.

name: François Boucher
bio: François Boucher was a French painter, draughtsman and etcher who worked in the Rococo style. Boucher is known for his idyllic and voluptuous paintings on classical themes, decorative allegories and pastoral scenes. He was perhaps the most celebrated painter and decorative artist of the 18th century.
whyTheyMatter: Perhaps the most celebrated painter and decorative artist of the 18th century.

name: Jean-Honoré Fragonard
bio: Jean-Honoré Fragonard was a French painter and printmaker whose late Rococo manner was distinguished by remarkable facility, exuberance and hedonism. One of the most prolific artists active in the last decades of the Ancien Régime, Fragonard produced more than 550 paintings, among his most popular works genre paintings conveying an atmosphere of intimacy and veiled eroticism.
whyTheyMatter: The exuberant final flourish of the Rococo in the last decades of the Ancien Régime.

name: Jacques-Louis David
bio: Jacques-Louis David was a French painter in the Neoclassical style, considered to be the preeminent painter of the era. In the 1780s, his cerebral brand of history painting marked a change in taste away from Rococo frivolity toward classical austerity, severity and heightened feeling, which harmonized with the moral climate of the final years of the Ancien Régime.
whyTheyMatter: The preeminent painter of Neoclassicism, who turned taste from Rococo frivolity to classical austerity.

name: Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres
bio: Jean-Auguste-Dominique Ingres was a French Neoclassical painter, profoundly influenced by past artistic traditions and aspiring to become the guardian of academic orthodoxy against the ascendant Romantic style. Although he considered himself a painter of history in the tradition of Nicolas Poussin and Jacques-Louis David, it is his portraits, both painted and drawn, that are recognized as his greatest legacy. His expressive distortions of form and space made him an important precursor of modern art, influencing Henri Matisse, Pablo Picasso and other modernists.
whyTheyMatter: Guardian of the classical line whose expressive distortions made him a precursor of modern art.

name: Angelica Kauffman
bio: Maria Anna Angelika Kauffmann, usually known in English as Angelica Kauffman, was a Swiss painter who had a successful career in London and Rome. Remembered primarily as a history painter, Kauffman was also a skilled portraitist, landscape and decoration painter. She was, along with Mary Moser, one of two female painters among the founding members of the Royal Academy of Arts in London in 1768.
whyTheyMatter: Pioneering Neoclassical history painter and a founding member of the Royal Academy.

name: Francisco Goya
bio: Francisco José de Goya y Lucientes was a Spanish romantic painter and printmaker, considered the most important Spanish artist of the late 18th and early 19th centuries. His paintings, drawings and engravings reflected contemporary historical upheavals and influenced important 19th- and 20th-century painters. Goya is often referred to as the last of the Old Masters and the first of the moderns.
whyTheyMatter: The last of the Old Masters and the first of the moderns.

name: J. M. W. Turner
bio: Joseph Mallord William Turner was an English Romantic painter, printmaker and watercolourist, known for his expressive colouring, imaginative landscapes and turbulent, often violent marine paintings. He left behind more than 550 oil paintings, 2,000 watercolours and 30,000 works on paper. He is today regarded as having elevated landscape painting to an eminence rivalling history painting, and his later works are seen as a significant precursor of the Impressionist and abstract art movements that arose after his death.
whyTheyMatter: Elevated landscape to the eminence of history painting and presaged Impressionism and abstraction.

name: Eugène Delacroix
bio: Ferdinand Victor Eugène Delacroix was a French Romantic artist regarded as the leader of the French Romantic school. In contrast to the Neoclassical perfectionism of his chief rival Ingres, Delacroix took his inspiration from the art of Rubens and painters of the Venetian Renaissance, with an attendant emphasis on colour and movement rather than clarity of outline and carefully modelled form. Dramatic and romantic content characterized the central themes of his maturity, and led him to travel in North Africa in search of the exotic.
whyTheyMatter: Leader of the French Romantic school, whose primacy of colour over line liberated later painting.

name: Caspar David Friedrich
bio: Caspar David Friedrich was a German Romantic landscape painter, generally considered the most important German artist of his generation. His often symbolic and anti-classical work conveys a subjective, emotional response to the natural world. Friedrich's paintings often set contemplative human figures silhouetted against night skies, morning mists, barren trees or Gothic ruins.
whyTheyMatter: Defined the Romantic sublime in landscape painting.

name: Gustave Courbet
bio: Jean Désiré Gustave Courbet was a French painter who led the Realism movement in 19th-century French painting. Committed to painting only what he could see, he rejected academic convention and the Romanticism of the previous generation of visual artists. His independence set an example that was important to later artists, such as the Impressionists and the Cubists.
whyTheyMatter: Led the Realism movement and made bold social statements through painting.

name: Jean-François Millet
bio: Jean-François Millet was a French painter and one of the founders of the Barbizon school in rural France. Millet is noted for his paintings of peasant farmers and can be categorized as part of the Realism art movement. Toward the end of his career he became increasingly interested in painting pure landscapes, and he is also noted for his pastels, Conté crayon drawings and etchings.
whyTheyMatter: Co-founder of the Barbizon school who gave dignity to peasant labour in paint.

name: Honoré Daumier
bio: Honoré-Victorin Daumier was a French painter, sculptor and printmaker whose many works offer commentary on the social and political life in France from the Revolution of 1830 to the fall of the Second French Empire in 1870. He earned a living producing caricatures and cartoons in newspapers and periodicals such as La Caricature and Le Charivari, for which he became well known in his lifetime.
whyTheyMatter: Unsparing observer and satirist of 19th-century French society.

name: Édouard Manet
bio: Édouard Manet was a French modernist painter. He was one of the first 19th-century artists to paint modern life, as well as a pivotal figure in the transition from Realism to Impressionism.
whyTheyMatter: Pivotal figure in the transition from Realism to Impressionism and painter of modern life.

name: Claude Monet
bio: Oscar-Claude Monet was a French painter and founder of Impressionism, seen as a key precursor to modernism in his attempts to paint nature as he perceived it. During his long career, he was the most consistent and prolific practitioner of Impressionism's philosophy of expressing one's perceptions of nature, especially as applied to plein air landscape painting. The term 'Impressionism' is derived from the title of his painting Impression, Sunrise, exhibited in 1874 at the First Impressionist Exhibition.
whyTheyMatter: Founder of Impressionism, which took its very name from his Impression, Sunrise.

name: Pierre-Auguste Renoir
bio: Pierre-Auguste Renoir was a leading French Impressionist artist. In his depiction of feminine beauty, Renoir has been described as 'the final representative of a tradition which runs directly from Rubens to Watteau'.
whyTheyMatter: Leading Impressionist celebrant of light, leisure and the human figure.

name: Edgar Degas
bio: Edgar Degas was a French Impressionist artist famous for his pastel drawings and oil paintings, especially identified with the subject of dance; more than half of his works depict dancers. Although Degas is regarded as one of the founders of Impressionism, he rejected the term, preferring to be called a realist, and did not paint outdoors as many Impressionists did. He was a superb draftsman, particularly masterly in depicting movement.
whyTheyMatter: A founder of Impressionism and its unrivalled draughtsman of movement.

name: Camille Pissarro
bio: Jacob Abraham Camille Pissarro was a Danish-French Impressionist and Neo-Impressionist painter born on the island of Saint Thomas. His importance resides in his contributions to both Impressionism and Post-Impressionism. Pissarro studied from great forerunners, including Gustave Courbet and Jean-Baptiste-Camille Corot, and later studied and worked alongside Georges Seurat and Paul Signac when he took on the Neo-Impressionist style at the age of 54.
whyTheyMatter: The patriarch who linked Impressionism to Post-Impressionism.

name: Berthe Morisot
bio: Berthe Marie Pauline Morisot was a French painter and printmaker, a member of the circle of painters in Paris who became known as the Impressionists. In 1864 she exhibited for the first time in the highly esteemed Paris Salon, and in 1874 she joined the 'rejected' Impressionists in the first of their own exhibitions. Morisot went on to participate in all but one of the following eight Impressionist exhibitions, between 1874 and 1886.
whyTheyMatter: Core member of the Impressionist circle from its first independent exhibition.

name: Vincent van Gogh
bio: Vincent Willem van Gogh was a Dutch Post-Impressionist painter who is among the most famous and influential figures in the history of Western art. In just over a decade he created about 2,100 artworks, including around 860 oil paintings, most of them in the last two years of his life. His landscapes, still lifes, portraits and self-portraits are characterised by bold colours and dramatic, impulsive and expressive brushwork that contributed to the foundations of modern art.
whyTheyMatter: His bold colour and expressive brushwork contributed to the foundations of modern art.

name: Paul Cézanne
bio: Paul Cézanne was a French Post-Impressionist painter whose work introduced new modes of representation and influenced avant-garde artistic movements of the early 20th century. Cézanne is said to have formed the bridge between late 19th-century Impressionism and early 20th-century Cubism.
whyTheyMatter: The bridge between Impressionism and Cubism.

name: Paul Gauguin
bio: Eugène Henri Paul Gauguin was a French painter, sculptor, printmaker, ceramist and writer, whose work has been primarily associated with the Post-Impressionist and Symbolist movements. While only moderately successful during his lifetime, Gauguin has since been recognized for his experimental use of color and Synthetist style that were distinct from Impressionism.
whyTheyMatter: His experimental colour and Synthetist style opened new paths beyond Impressionism.

name: Georges Seurat
bio: Georges Pierre Seurat was a French post-Impressionist artist. He devised the painting techniques known as chromoluminarism and pointillism, and used conté crayon for drawings on paper with a rough surface.
whyTheyMatter: Deviser of pointillism and chromoluminarism, founding techniques of Neo-Impressionism.

name: Edvard Munch
bio: Edvard Munch was a Norwegian painter whose 1893 work The Scream has become one of the most iconic and acclaimed images in all of Western art. Studying at the Royal School of Art and Design in Kristiania (Oslo), Munch began to live a bohemian life under the influence of the nihilist Hans Jæger, who urged him to paint his own emotional and psychological state; from this emerged his distinctive 'soul painting' style. In Paris he learned much from Gauguin, van Gogh and Toulouse-Lautrec, especially their use of color.
whyTheyMatter: Painter of The Scream, whose 'soul painting' fed directly into Expressionism.

name: Ernst Ludwig Kirchner
bio: Ernst Ludwig Kirchner was a German expressionist painter and printmaker, one of the founders of the artists group Die Brücke or 'The Bridge', a key group leading to the foundation of Expressionism in 20th-century art. His work was branded as 'degenerate' by the Nazis in 1933, and in 1937 more than 600 of his works were sold or destroyed.
whyTheyMatter: Co-founder of Die Brücke, the wellspring of German Expressionism.

name: Wassily Kandinsky
bio: Wassily Wassilyevich Kandinsky was a Russian painter and art theorist active in Germany during the late Belle Époque and Interwar eras. Kandinsky is generally credited as one of the pioneers of abstraction in Western art. Born in Moscow, he began painting studies at the age of 30.
whyTheyMatter: One of the pioneers of abstraction in Western art.

name: Egon Schiele
bio: Egon Schiele was an Austrian Expressionist painter whose work is noted for its intensity and its raw sexuality, and for the many self-portraits the artist produced, including nude self-portraits. The twisted body shapes and the expressive line that characterise his paintings and drawings mark him as an early exponent of Expressionism. Gustav Klimt was a mentor to Schiele.
whyTheyMatter: The raw, linear intensity of Viennese Expressionism.

name: Pablo Picasso
bio: Pablo Ruiz Picasso was a Spanish painter and sculptor who spent most of his adult life in France. One of the most influential artists of the 20th century, he is known for co-founding the Cubist movement, the invention of constructed sculpture, the co-invention of collage, and for the wide variety of styles that he helped develop and explore. Among his most famous works are the proto-Cubist Les Demoiselles d'Avignon (1907) and the anti-war painting Guernica (1937).
whyTheyMatter: Co-founder of Cubism and one of the most influential artists of the 20th century.

name: Georges Braque
bio: Georges Braque was a major 20th-century French painter, collagist, draughtsman, printmaker and sculptor. His most notable contributions were in his alliance with Fauvism from 1905, and the role he played in the development of Cubism. Braque's work between 1908 and 1912 is closely associated with that of his colleague Pablo Picasso; their respective Cubist works were indistinguishable for many years.
whyTheyMatter: Co-developer of Cubism alongside Picasso.

name: Juan Gris
bio: José Victoriano González-Pérez, better known as Juan Gris, was a Spanish painter born in Madrid who lived and worked in France for most of his active period. Closely connected to the innovative artistic genre Cubism, his works are among the movement's most distinctive.
whyTheyMatter: Painter of some of Cubism's most distinctive works.

name: Salvador Dalí
bio: Salvador Dalí was a Spanish surrealist artist renowned for his technical skill, precise draftsmanship, and the striking and bizarre images in his work. Born in Figueres and trained in fine arts in Madrid, he moved closer to Surrealism in the late 1920s and joined the Surrealist group in 1929, soon becoming one of its leading exponents. His best-known work, The Persistence of Memory, was completed in August 1931.
whyTheyMatter: Surrealism's most famous image-maker, painter of The Persistence of Memory.

name: René Magritte
bio: René François Ghislain Magritte was a Belgian surrealist artist known for his depictions of familiar objects in unfamiliar, unexpected contexts, which often provoked questions about the nature and boundaries of reality and representation. His imagery has influenced pop art, minimalist art, and conceptual art.
whyTheyMatter: Turned familiar objects into enduring riddles about reality and representation.

name: Max Ernst
bio: Max Ernst was a German-born painter, sculptor, printmaker, graphic artist and poet. A prolific artist, Ernst was a primary pioneer of the Dada movement and surrealism in Europe. He had no formal artistic training, but his experimental attitude toward the making of art resulted in his invention of frottage and grattage, techniques that reveal images from rubbed and scraped surfaces.
whyTheyMatter: Primary pioneer of Dada and Surrealism, inventor of frottage and grattage.

name: Joan Miró
bio: Joan Miró i Ferrà was a Catalan painter, sculptor and ceramist from Spain. Earning international acclaim, his work has been interpreted as Surrealism but with a personal style, sometimes also veering into Fauvism and Expressionism. He was notable for his interest in the unconscious or the subconscious mind, reflected in his re-creation of the childlike.
whyTheyMatter: Surrealism's poet of signs, colour and the unconscious.

name: Jackson Pollock
bio: Paul Jackson Pollock was an American painter and a major figure in the abstract expressionist movement. He was widely noticed for his 'drip technique' of pouring or splashing liquid household paint onto a horizontal surface, enabling him to view and paint his canvases from all angles. The method was called all-over painting and action painting, because he covered the entire canvas and used the force of his whole body to paint.
whyTheyMatter: His drip paintings made action painting the emblem of Abstract Expressionism.

name: Mark Rothko
bio: Mark Rothko was a Latvian-born American abstract painter, best known for his color field paintings that depicted irregular and painterly rectangular regions of color, which he produced from 1949 to 1970. Although Rothko did not personally subscribe to any one school, he is associated with the American abstract expressionism movement of modern art.
whyTheyMatter: His floating fields of colour define the meditative wing of Abstract Expressionism.

name: Willem de Kooning
bio: Willem de Kooning was a Dutch-American abstract expressionist artist. Born in Rotterdam, he moved to the United States in 1926, becoming a U.S. citizen in 1962. In the years after World War II, de Kooning painted in a style that came to be referred to as abstract expressionism or 'action painting', and was part of a group of artists that came to be known as the New York School, alongside Jackson Pollock, Mark Rothko and others.
whyTheyMatter: Leading action painter of the New York School.

name: Andy Warhol
bio: Andy Warhol was an American artist and filmmaker, widely regarded as the most important artist of the second half of the 20th century. A leading figure in the Pop art movement, his work explores the relationship between advertising, consumerism, mass media, and celebrity culture. His embrace of mechanical reproduction challenged traditional boundaries between high and low culture.
whyTheyMatter: Pop art's defining figure, widely regarded as the most important artist of the later 20th century.

name: Roy Lichtenstein
bio: Roy Fox Lichtenstein was an American artist and a leading figure of the Pop Art movement, best known for his large-scale paintings inspired by comic books, advertisements, and mass-produced imagery. His art is represented in major museum collections worldwide, and he remains one of the most influential and recognizable artists of the 20th century.
whyTheyMatter: Turned the comic strip into monumental painting.

name: Richard Hamilton
bio: Richard William Hamilton was an English painter and collage artist. His 1955 exhibition Man, Machine and Motion and his 1956 collage Just what is it that makes today's homes so different, so appealing?, produced for the This Is Tomorrow exhibition of the Independent Group in London, are considered by critics and historians to be among the earliest works of pop art. A major retrospective of his work was held at Tate Modern in 2014.
whyTheyMatter: Creator of what critics consider among the earliest works of pop art.

name: David Hockney
bio: David Hockney was an English painter, stage designer and photographer. As an important contributor to the pop art movement of the 1960s, he is considered one of the most influential British artists of the 20th and 21st centuries. He was known for his vivid, stylised realism and championed figurative work, often in a bold style, during a period when abstract art predominated.
whyTheyMatter: Pop art pioneer who kept figurative painting vital for six decades.

name: Gerhard Richter
bio: Gerhard Richter is a German visual artist who has produced abstract as well as photorealistic paintings, in addition to photographs and glass pieces. He is widely regarded as one of the most important contemporary German artists, and several of his works have set record prices at auction, with him being the most expensive living painter at one time.
whyTheyMatter: Restless master of both photorealism and abstraction, among the most important contemporary German artists.

name: Jean-Michel Basquiat
bio: Jean-Michel Basquiat was an American artist who rose to success during the 1980s as part of the Neo-expressionism movement. He first achieved notoriety in the late 1970s as part of the graffiti duo SAMO in Manhattan; by the early 1980s his paintings were being exhibited in galleries and museums internationally. At 21, Basquiat became the youngest artist to ever take part in Documenta in Kassel, Germany.
whyTheyMatter: Took painting from New York's streets to the center of contemporary art.

name: Anselm Kiefer
bio: Anselm Kiefer is a German painter and sculptor who studied with Peter Dreher and Horst Antes at the end of the 1960s. His works incorporate materials such as straw, ash, clay, lead, and shellac. The poems of Paul Celan have played a role in developing Kiefer's themes of German history and the horrors of the Holocaust, as have the spiritual concepts of Kabbalah.
whyTheyMatter: Confronts German history and myth on a monumental painterly scale.

name: Lucian Freud
bio: Lucian Michael Freud was a British painter and draughtsman, specialising in figurative art, who is known as one of the foremost 20th-century English portraitists. His early career was influenced by surrealism and expressionism, but by the early 1950s his often stark and alienated paintings tended towards realism. His paintings, completed over a 60-year career, are mostly of friends and intimates.
whyTheyMatter: One of the foremost figurative portraitists of the postwar era.
